alsoKnownAs: NOTE — trade-name confusion: 'Bumblebee Catfish' is also applied to the Asian Bumblebee Catfish (Pseudomystus siamensis, fw-fish-144), a larger, more territorial Bagridae species reaching 15–20 cm. M. iheringi (SA) stays 6–8 cm and is far more peaceful. Always verify family (Pseudopimelodidae = SA; Bagridae = Asian) before purchase.

nativeRange.habitat: Swift-flowing rivers and streams over rock, gravel, and sand; clear, well-oxygenated water with moderate to strong current. Orinoco basin including the Turmero River (Aragua, Venezuela).

decorPreferences: caves and rocky crevices for daytime sheltering
robust plants (Java fern, Anubias) for shading
moderate water flow replicating riverine habitat

diet.notes: Carnivore-leaning omnivore. In the wild feeds primarily on insects (especially ants) and other invertebrates. In captivity readily accepts high-quality sinking catfish pellets, wafers, frozen or freeze-dried bloodworms, brine shrimp, tubifex, and daphnia. Blanched vegetables and algae wafers accepted but protein foods should dominate. Feed at lights-out or under dim blue moonlight when the fish actively forages. Avoid overfeeding — every 1–2 days is sufficient for adults.

goodWith: medium-sized tetras (black skirt, bleeding heart, rummy nose)
avoidWith: Asian Bumblebee Catfish (Pseudomystus siamensis, fw-fish-144) — larger (15–20 cm), Bagridae family, more territorial; often mislabeled as the same species in the trade; incompatible care requirements and size mismatch
small nano fish and fry (neon tetras, ember tetras, small rasboras) — risk of predation at night
dwarf shrimp (cherry, amano, crystal) — will be eaten

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase specimen photo for Microglanis iheringi (species ID 47758). Check availability and license before use.

summary: A small (6–8 cm), black-and-yellow banded South American catfish from the Orinoco basin. Nocturnal and secretive, ideal for peaceful community tanks. Often confused in trade with the larger Asian Bumblebee Catfish (Pseudomystus siamensis, Bagridae) — verify family on purchase. Scaleless; copper-sensitive.
careNotes:
The South American Bumblebee Catfish is one of the most distinctive dwarf catfish available in the hobby, bearing vivid black-and-yellow (occasionally dark-brown-and-tan) banding that earned it the common name. Adults reach only 6–8 cm, making it suitable for tanks from 75 L upward. Being scaleless (Siluriformes lack true scales), it is sensitive to copper-based medications — never use copper treatments in a tank housing this species.

This species is strictly nocturnal and can be almost invisible during daylight hours, spending the day wedged into a cave, under driftwood, or behind rockwork. Aquarists may go weeks without observing it if hiding spots are plentiful. Feed at lights-out or under blue moonlight to ensure it receives adequate nutrition; sinking foods that remain on the substrate overnight work well. In well-planted, cave-rich tanks it is possible to keep two or three together without significant conflict, though a single specimen is equally content and easier to monitor.

Water quality is important — as a riverine species it is intolerant of elevated nitrates. Regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly) and an efficient filter maintaining moderate flow are recommended. Soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 5–15) most closely matches its Orinoco basin origin.

TRADE-NAME CONFUSION: 'Bumblebee Catfish' is applied to at least two distinct species in the hobby. The South American species (M. iheringi, Pseudopimelodidae) stays 6–8 cm and is relatively peaceful — it will consume small fry and dwarf shrimp but poses no threat to medium tankmates. The Asian Bumblebee Catfish (Pseudomystus siamensis, Bagridae, fw-fish-144) grows to 15–20 cm, is more territorial and predatory, and requires a considerably larger tank with different tankmates. Always verify the family (Pseudopimelodidae vs. Bagridae) and origin before purchase.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is extremely rare; no reliably documented home-aquarium successes have been widely reported as of 2026. Females can produce up to 650 eggs per clutch in the wild. Attempted captive spawning would likely require simulation of seasonal conditions (cooler temperature dip, increased water flow, slightly lowered pH), appropriately sized spawning caves, and live food conditioning. Not a realistic breeding project for most hobbyists.

primary.name: FishBase — Microglanis iheringi (Species ID 47758)
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and biological reference. Provided: family (Pseudopimelodidae), order (Siluriformes), native range (Turmero River basin, Venezuela), maximum SL 6.0 cm, water parameters (24–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0, dH 5–12), diet (insects, ants), IUCN Least Concern (2020). Size range extended to 8 cm per ScotCat factsheet (8.5 cm max) and hobbyist consensus of 6–8 cm.

additional.name: ScotCat Factsheet — Microglanis iheringi Gomes, 1946
additional.notes: Specialist catfish reference. Provided max size 8.5 cm, temperature 21–25°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 10–18 dGH, coloration description (dark brown markings on light brown background, bar-shaped caudal blotch), note that species consumes small livebearer fry, and behavioural notes on extreme nocturnality.

additional.name: Tankarium — Bumblebee Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist care guide. Provided tank size recommendation (20 gal / ~75 L minimum), water parameters (70–77°F / 21–25°C, pH 6.5–7.5, gH 8–12), diet guidance, compatibility notes, and documentation of trade-name confusion across three 'bumblebee catfish' species (SA 3–3.5 in, Asian 6 in, Giant 10 in). Breeding described as very difficult with no documented US captive cases.

additional.name: AquariumSource — Bumblebee Catfish Care
additional.notes: Hobbyist reference confirming 3-inch typical adult size, 4–5 year lifespan, 20-gallon minimum tank, omnivorous diet (sinking pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp), nocturnal behavior, compatibility with rasboras, corydoras, kuhli loaches, and note that Asian bumblebee catfish (Pseudomystus siamensis) is often confused with this species.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudomystus siamensis (Asian Bumblebee Catfish)
additional.notes: Used to document the contrasting species. Confirmed Pseudomystus siamensis reaches 15 cm SL, family Bagridae (not Pseudopimelodidae), more territorial disposition especially toward other bottom-dwellers, distinguishable from M. iheringi by nasal barbels in front of eyes. Key reference for the trade-name confusion advisory.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliably documented home-aquarium breeding successes. Wild females produce ~650 eggs per clutch. Captive attempts would require seasonal conditioning, spawning caves, live food, and water parameter manipulation — considered impractical for most hobbyists.
